titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender, circa November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir WilfrId Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/76

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
A letter has come in asking
for someone to be nominated
as a Lt Colonel, but a
Staff officer is not eligible
for the appointment ! I wonder who devised
this new theory & if I am
the unwitting cause ! Boylan Smith & I have had
some amusing correspondence
apropos of the sanitary arrange-
ments of the office. He made
various proposals that the G
branch ougth with their big
brains to be able to dial with
the enemy — b flats — etc etc I replied that we should be
glad for his assistance & that he
should come & sleep in the office
disguised as a staff officer. There was further amusing play
between us ending up in his
presumed decease whereon we pent
the following of obituary notices:—
G.) This was my best & I've
forgotten it !
A.) Here lies the body of Boylan Smith
We have buried him deep with toil &
grief.
(ADMS who has rows with him
about smoking & ventilation).
A beautiful end and a happy release
now I can smoke the pipe of peace
( The Units whom he used to
inspect.)
Tho' tins & creosol we now
do not lack
May Heaven preserve him & let but not
send him back.
